author: John Chrysostom
book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.

classification.cautionReason: No major mainstream heresy warning is attached by the preview classifier.

lineage: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.

ccel.title: NPNF1-10. St. Chrysostom: Homilies on the Gospel of Saint Matthew

source.title: NPNF1-10. St. Chrysostom: Homilies on the Gospel of Saint Matthew

title: Matthew II. 2.

source.title: NPNF1-10. St. Chrysostom: Homilies on the Gospel of Saint Matthew

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: “And when they were come into the house, they saw the young Child with Mary His mother.”

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: How then saith Luke, that He was lying in the manger?

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Because at the birth indeed she presently laid Him there (for, as was not unlikely, in that large assemblage for the taxing, they could find no house; which Luke also signifies, by saying, “Because there was no room, she laid Him” there); but afterwards she took Him up, and held Him on her knees.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: For no sooner was she arrived at Bethlehem than she brought her pangs to an end,that thou mayest thence also learn the whole dispensation, and that these things were not done at random, or by chance, but that they all were in course of accomplishment, according to some Divine foreknowledge, and prophetic order.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: But what was it that induced them to worship?

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: For neither was the Virgin conspicuous, nor the house distinguished, nor was any other of the things which they saw apt to amaze or attract them.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Yet they not only worship, but also “open their treasures,” and “offer gifts;” and gifts, not as to a man, but as to God.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: For the frankincense and the myrrh were a symbol of this.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: What then was their inducement?

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: That which wrought upon them to set out from home and to come so long a journey; and this was both the star, and the illumination wrought of God in their mind, guiding them by little and little to the more perfect knowledge.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: For, surely, had it not been so, all that was in sight being ordinary, they would not have shown so great honor.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Therefore none of the outward circumstances was great in that instance, but it was a manger, and a shed, and a mother in poor estate; to set before thine eyes, naked and bare, those wise men’s love of wisdom,and to prove to thee, that not as mere man they approached Him, but as a God, and Benefactor.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Wherefore neither were they offended by ought of what they saw outwardly, but even worshipped, and brought gifts; gifts not only free from Judaical grossness, in that they sacrificed not sheep and calves, but also coming nigh to the self-devotion of the Church, for it was knowledge and obedience and love that they offered unto Him.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: “And being warned of God in a dream that they should not return unto Herod, they departed into their own country another way.”

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: See from this also their faith, how they were not offended, but are docile, and considerate; neither are they troubled, nor reason with themselves, saying, “And yet, if this Child be great, and hath any might, what need of flight, and of a clandestine retreat? and wherefore can it be, that when we have come openly and with boldness, and have stood against so great a people, and against a king’s madness, the angel sends us out of the city as runaways and fugitives?”

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: But none of these things did they either say or think.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: For this most especially belongs to faith, not to seek an account of what is enjoined, but merely to obey the commandments laid upon us.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: “And when they were departed, behold, an angel appeareth to Joseph in a dream, saying, Arise, and take the young Child and His mother, and flee into Egypt.”

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: There is something here worth inquiring into, both touching the magi, and touching the Child; for if even they were not troubled, but received all with faith, it is worthy of examination on our part, why they and the young Child are not preserved, continuing there, but they as fugitives go into Persia, He with His mother into Egypt.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: But what? should He have fallen into the hands of Herod, and having fallen, not have been cut off?

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Nay, He would not have been thought to have taken flesh upon Him; the greatness of the Economy would not have been believed.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: For if, while these things are taking place, and many circumstances are being ordered mysteriously after the manner of men, some have dared to say that His assumption of our fleshis a fable; in what degree of impiety would they not have been wrecked had He done all in a manner becoming His Godhead, and according to His own power?

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: As to the wise men, He sends them off quickly, at once both commissioning them as teachers to the land of the Persians, and at the same time intercepting the madness of the king, that he might learn that he was attempting things impossible, and might quench his wrath, and desist from this his vain labor.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: For not alone openly to subdue His enemies, but also to deceive them with ease, is worthy of His power.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Thus, for example, He deceived the Egyptians also in the case of the Jews, and having power to transfer their wealth openly into the hands of the Hebrews, He bids them do this secretly and with craft; and this surely, not less than the other miracles, made Him an object of terror to His enemies.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: At least, they of Ascalon, and all the rest, when they had taken the ark, and being smitten, did after that devise their countrymen not to fight, nor to set themselves against Him, with the other miracles brought this also forward, saying, “Wherefore harden ye your hearts, as Egypt and Pharaoh hardened? when He had mocked them, did He not after that send forth His people, and they departed?”

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Now this they said, as accounting this fresh one not inferior to those other signs that had been done openly, towards the demonstration of His power, and of His greatness.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: And the like ensued on this occasion too; a thing sufficient to astonish the tyrant.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: For consider what it was natural for Herod to feel, and how his very breath would be stopped, deceived as he was by the wise men, and thus laughed to scorn.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: For what, if he did not become better?

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: It is not His fault, who marvellously ordered all this, but it is the excess of Herod’s madness, not yielding even to those things which had virtueto have persuaded him, and deterred him from his wickedness, but going on still further, to receive a yet sharper punishment for folly so great.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: But wherefore, it may be said, is the young Child sent into Egypt?

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: In the first place, the evangelist himself hath mentioned the cause, saying, “That it might be fulfilled, Out of Egypt have I called my Son.”

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: And at the same time beginnings of fair hopes were thenceforth proclaimed before to the world.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: That is, since Babylon and Egypt, most in the whole earth, were burnt up with the flame of ungodliness, He, signifying from the first that He means to correct and amend both, and inducing men hereby to expect His bounties in regard of the whole world likewise, sent to the one the wise men, the other He Himself visited with His mother.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: And besides what I have said, there is another lesson also, which we are hereby taught, tending not slightly to true self-command in us.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Of what kind then is it?

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: To look from the beginning for temptations and plots.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: See, for instance, how this was the case even at once from His swaddling clothes.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Thus you see at His birth, first a tyrant raging, then flight ensuing, and departure beyond the border; and for no crime His mother is exiled into the land of the barbarians: that thou, hearing these things (supposing thee thought worthy to minister to any spiritual matter, and then to see thyself suffering incurable ills, and enduring countless dangers), shouldest not be greatly troubled, nor say, “What can this be? yet surely I ought to be crowned and celebrated, and be glorious and illustrious for fulfilling the Lord’s commandment:” but that having this example, thou mightest bear all things nobly, knowing that this especially is the order of all things spiritual, to have everywhere temptations in the same lot with them.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: See at least how this is the case not only with regard to the mother of the young Child, but also of those barbarians; since they for their part retire secretly in the condition of fugitives; and she again, who had never passed over the threshold of her house, is commanded to undergo so long a journey of affliction, on account of this wonderful birth, and her spiritual travail.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: And behold a wonder again.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Palestine plots, and Egypt receives and preserves Him that is the object of the plots.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: For, as it appears, not only in the instance of the sons of the patriarchdid types take place, but also in our Lord’s own case.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: In many instances, we are sure, His doings at that time were prophetic declarations of what was to happen afterwards; as, for example, in the matter of the ass and the colt.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Now the angel having thus appeared, talks not with Mary, but with Joseph; and what saith he?

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: “Arise, and take the young Child and His mother.”

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Here, he saith not any more, “thy wife,” but “His mother.”

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: For after that the birth had taken place, and the suspicion was done away, and the husband appeased, thenceforth the angel talks openly, calling neither child nor wife his, but “take the young Child and His mother, and flee into Egypt;” and he mentions the cause of the flight: “For Herod,” saith he, “will seek the young Child’s life.”

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Joseph, when he had heard these things, was not offended, nether did he say, “The thing is hard to understand: Didst thou not say just now, that He should save His people?’ and now He saves not even Himself: but we must fly, and go far from home, and be a long time away: the facts are contrary to the promise.”

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Nay, none of these things doth he say (for the man was faithful): neither is he curious about the time of his return; and this though the angel had put it indefinitely thus: “Be thou there until I tell thee.”

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: But nevertheless, not even at this did he shudder, but submits and obeys, undergoing all the trials with joy.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: And this because God, who is full of love to man, did with these hardships mingle things pleasant also; which indeed is His way with regard to all the saints, making neither their dangers nor their refreshment continual, but weaving the life of all righteous men, out of both the one and the other.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: This very thing He did here also: for consider, Joseph saw the Virgin with child; this cast him into agitation and the utmost trouble, for he was suspecting the damsel of adultery.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: But straightway the angel was at hand to do away his suspicion, and remove his fears; and seeing the young child born, he reaped the greatest joy.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Again, this joy no trifling danger succeeds, the city being troubled, and the king in his madness seeking after Him that was born.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: But this trouble was again succeeded by another joy; the star, and the adoration of the wise men.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Again, after this pleasure, fear and danger; “For Herod,” saith he, “is seeking the young Child’s life,” and He must needs fly and withdraw Himself as any mortal might: the working of miracles not being seasonable as yet.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: For if from His earliest infancy He had shown forth wonders, He would not have been accounted a Man.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Because of this, let me add, neither is a temple framed at once; but a regular conception takes place, and a time of nine months, and pangs, and a delivery, and giving suck, and silence for so long a space, and He awaits the age proper to manhood; that by all means acceptance might be won for the mystery of His Economy.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: “But wherefore then,” one may say, “were even these signs wrought at the beginning?”

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: For His mother’s sake; for the sake of Joseph and of Simeon, who was presently to depart; for the sake of the shepherds and of the wise men; for the sake of the Jews.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Since they, had they been willing to mind diligently what was taking place, would from this event also have reaped no small advantage in regard of what was to come.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: But if the prophets do not mention what relates to the wise men, be not troubled; for they neither foretold all things, nor were they silent touching all.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: For as without any warning to see those things coming to pass, would naturally occasion much astonishment and trouble; so also to have been informed of all would dispose the hearer to sleep, and would have left nothing for the evangelists to add.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: And if the Jews should raise a question touching the prophecy, and say, that the words, “Out of Egypt have I called my Son,” were uttered concerning themselves; we would tell them, This is a law of prophecy, that in many cases much that is spoken of one set of persons is fulfilled in another; of which kind is that which is said touching Simeon and Levi, “I will divide them,” saith He, “in Jacob, and scatter them in Israel.”

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: And yet not in themselves did this come to pass, but in their descendants; and Noah’s saying again about Canaan, came to pass in the Gibeonites, Canaan’s descendants.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: And that concerning Jacob one may see to have so come to pass; for those blessings which say, “Be lord over thy brother, and let thy father’s sons worship thee,”had no accomplishment in himself (how could they, he being in fear and trembling, and worshipping his brother over and over again?), but in his offspring they had.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: The very same may be said in this case also.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: For which may be called the truer son of God, he that worships a calf, and is joined to Baalpeorand sacrifices his sons to devils? or He that is a Son by nature, and honors Him that begat Him?

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: So that, except this man had come, the prophecy would not have received, its due fulfillment.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: It is worth observing, too, that the evangelist intimates the same by the phrase, “that it might be fulfilled;” implying that it would not have been fulfilled, unless He had come.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: And this makes the Virgin also in no common degree glorious and distinguished; that the very thing which was the whole people’s special endowment in the way of praise, she also might thenceforth have for her own.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: I mean, that whereas they were proud of their coming up from Egypt, and used to boast of it (which indeed the prophet also was hinting at, when he said, “Have I not brought up the strangers from Cappadocia, and the Assyrians from the pit”), He makes this pre-eminence belong to the Virgin likewise.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Rather, however, both the people and the patriarch, going down thither, and coming up thence, were together completing the type of this His return.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Thus, as they went down to avoid death by famine, so He death by conspiracy.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: But whereas they on their arrival were for the time delivered from the famine, this man, when He had gone down, sanctified the whole land, by setting His foot thereon.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: At least it is observable how, in the midst of His humiliations, the tokens of His Godhead are disclosed.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Thus, first of all, the angel saying, “Flee into Egypt,” did not promise to journey with them, either in their descent or return; intimating that they have a great fellow-traveller, the Child that had been born; such an one as actually changed all things immediately on His appearing, and wrought so that His enemies should minister in many ways to this Economy.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Thus magi and barbarians, leaving the superstition of their fathers, are come to worship: thus Augustus ministers to the birth at Bethlehem by the decree for the taxing; Egypt receives and preserves Him, driven from His home, and plotted against, and obtains a sort of first impulse towards her union unto Him; so that when in after-time she should hear Him preached by the apostles, she might have this at least to glory of, as having received Him first.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: And yet this privilege did belong unto Palestine alone; but the second proved more fervent than the first.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: And now, shouldest thou come unto the desert of Egypt, thou wilt see this desert become better than any paradise, and ten thousand choirs of angels in human forms, and nations of martyrs, and companies of virgins, and all the devil’s tyranny put down, while Christ’s kingdom shines forth in its brightness.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: And the mother of poets, and wise men, and magicians,were but inventions of sottish old women, but the real philosophy, and worthy of heaven, is this, which was declared unto them by the fishermen.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: And for this very cause, together with their so great exactness in doctrine, they exhibit also by their life that extreme seriousness.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: For when they have stripped themselves of all that they have, and are crucified to the whole world, they urge their course on again yet farther, using the labor of their body for the nourishment of them that be in need.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: For neither, because they fast and watch, do they think it meet to be idle by day; but their nights they spend in the holy hymns and in vigils, and their days in prayers, and at the same time in laboring with their own hands imitating the zeal of the apostle.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: For if he when the whole world was looking unto him for the sake of nourishing them that were in need, both occupied a workshop, and practised a craft, and being thus employed did not so much as sleep by night; how much more, say they, is it meet that we, who have taken up our abode in the wilderness, and have nothing to do with the turmoils in the cities, should use the leisure of our quiet for spiritual labors!

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Let us then be ashamed all of us, both they that are rich, and they that are poor, when those having nothing at all but a body only and hands, force their way on and strive eagerly to find thence a supply for the poor; while we, having endless stores within, touch not even our superfluities for these objects.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: What kind of plea shall we have then, I pray thee? and what sort of excuse?

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Yet further consider, how of old these Egyptians were both avaricious, and gluttonous, together with their other vices.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: For there were the flesh-potswhich the Jews remember; there, the great tyranny of the belly.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Nevertheless, having a willing mind, they changed: and having caught fire from Christ, they set off at once on their voyage towards heaven; and though more ardent than the rest of mankind, and more headstrong, both in anger, and in bodily pleasures, they imitate the incorporeal powers in meekness, and in the rest of that freedom from passions which pertains unto self-denial.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Now if any man hath been in the country, he knows what I say.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: But if he have never entered those tabernacles, let him call to mind him who even until now is in the mouths of all men,—him whom, after the apostles, Egypt brought forth,—the blessed and great Antony; and let him put it to himself, “This man, too, was born in the same country with Pharaoh; nevertheless he was not thereby damaged, but both had a divine vision vouchsafed him, and showed forth such a life as the laws of Christ require.”

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: And this any man shall know perfectly, when he hath read the book that contains the history of that man’s life;in which also he will perceive much prophecy.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: I allude to his prediction about those infected with the errors of Arius, and his statement of the mischief that would arise from them; God even then having shown them to him, and sketched out before his eyes all that was coming.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: A thing which most especially (among the rest) serves to demonstrate the truth, that no person, belonging to the heresies without, hath such a man to mention.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: But, not to depend on us for this information, look earnestly into what is written in that book, and ye will learn all exactly, and thence be instructed in much self-denial.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: And this advice I give, that we not merely peruse what is written there, but that we also emulate it, and make neither place, nor education, nor forefathers’ wickedness an excuse.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: For if we will take heed to ourselves, none of these things shall be an hindrance to us, since even Abraham had an ungodly father,but he inherited not his wickedness; and Hezekiah, Ahaz: yet nevertheless he became dear to God.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: And Joseph too when in the midst of Egypt, adorned himself with the crowns of temperance; and the Three Children no less in the midst of Babylon, and of the palace, when a table like those at Sybaris was set before them, showed the highest self-denial; and Moses also in Egypt, and Paul in the whole world; but nothing was to any one of these an hindrance in the race of virtue.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Let us then, bearing in mind all these things, put out of the way these our superfluous pleas and excuses, and apply ourselves to those toils which the cause of virtue requires.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: For thus shall we both attract to ourselves more favor from God, and persuade Him to assist us in our struggles, and we shall obtain the eternal blessings; unto which God grant that we may all attain, by the grace and love towards man of our Lord Jesus Christ, to whom be glory and victory for ever and ever.
